title: Apply: Executive Branch
description: Apply your knowledge about the executive branch, including the founders' vision and the scope and limits to presidential power, in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Executive Branch—The Scope and Limits of Presidential Power

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Arthur Schlesinger’s *The Imperial Presidency* (1973).**

Both Lincoln and Roosevelt undertook acts they knew to be beyond the Constitution. Both did so in times of [[☃ definition 1]] crisis when the life of the nation seemed truly at stake. Both acted, knowingly or not, on Locke’s doctrine of emergency [[☃ definition 2]], trusting that Congress would eventually approve their actions.

**Which statement best summarizes Schlesinger’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a special power or right that someone has

choices.content: Presidents have always respected the separation of powers and have only taken more power when Congress approved their actions in advance.
Presidents like Lincoln and Roosevelt should be condemned for taking actions that were not permitted by the U.S. Constitution.
Presidential attempts to take more power have always been prevented or limited by Congress.
Presidents have extended their powers beyond what is allowed in the Constitution in order to deal with major crises.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 70 by Alexander Hamilton. In this essay, Hamilton presents arguments for the structure of the executive branch.**

The ingredients which constitute energy in the Executive are, first, unity; secondly, duration; thirdly, an adequate provision for its support; fourthly, competent powers. . . .

Those politicians and statesmen who have been the most celebrated for the soundness of their principles and for the justice of their views, have declared in favor of a single Executive and a numerous legislature. . . .

That unity is [[☃ definition 1]] to energy will not be disputed. Decision, activity, secrecy, and [[☃ definition 2]] will generally characterize the proceedings of one man in a much [[☃ definition 3]] degree than the proceedings of any greater number [of men]; and in proportion as the number [of men] is increased, these qualities will be diminished . . .

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: encouraging of; tending to make something happen

choices.content: Numerous people should run the executive branch because a single person would not have the energy required to govern effectively.
One person should run the executive branch because a single executive would be able to act quickly and decisively.
Three people should run the executive branch because its power to negotiate treaties with other countries should be divided.
The executive should be a single person because it would be too complicated for Congress if they have to negotiate with multiple people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This political cartoon, which was published in 1912, shows Theodore Roosevelt on the left and the ghost of George Washington on the right.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: "Anti-Third Term Principle," by Clifford K. Berryman, 1912. Source: [National Archives](https://www.archives.gov/historical-docs/todays-doc/?dod-date=227)

options.alt: The political cartoon shows Theodore Roosevelt on the left. He is holding a piece of paper that says, "My policies, my platforms, my issues." He is saying, "Thought I'd dodged that." He is looking over his shoulder at the ghost of George Washington. George Washington is pointing at Roosevelt and has "Anti-Third Term Principle" written across his chest.

choices.content: Presidential power should be restrained by term limits.
Presidents after George Washington should try to copy his foreign policy.
Presidents should seek a third term only if their policies and platforms require it.
President Washington was wrong to step down after only two terms in office.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This political cartoon, which was published in 1912, shows Theodore Roosevelt on the left and the ghost of George Washington on the right.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which constitutional amendment is most relevant to the topic of the cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: "Anti-Third Term Principle," by Clifford K. Berryman, 1912. Source: [National Archives](https://www.archives.gov/historical-docs/todays-doc/?dod-date=227)

options.alt: The political cartoon shows Theodore Roosevelt on the left. He is holding a piece of paper that says, "My policies, my platforms, my issues." He is saying, "Thought I'd dodged that." He is looking over his shoulder at the ghost of George Washington. George Washington is pointing at Roosevelt and has "Anti-Third Term Principle" written across his chest.

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-First Amendment
The Twenty-Second Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which scenario would most likely be considered a violation of the Twenty-Second Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A sitting president commits troops to a military conflict without a declaration of war from Congress.
A member of Congress runs for a sixth term in office after receiving support from her constituents.
A sitting president wins a third term in office after overwhelming support from the American public.
Congress blocks an executive order after passing a bill declaring all executive orders null and void.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article by Luther Martin in the Baltimore *Maryland Gazette*. It was published on January 29, 1788.**

It was further observed, that the only appearance of [[☃ definition 1]] in the president, which the system holds up to our view, is the provision for impeachment; but that when we reflect that he cannot be impeached but by the house of delegates, and that the members of this house are [[☃ definition 3]] dependent upon, and unduly under the influence of the president, by being appointable to offices of which he has the sole nomination, so that without his favor and [[☃ definition 2]], they cannot obtain them, there is little reason to believe that a majority will ever [[☃ definition 6]] in impeaching the president, let his conduct be ever so [[☃ definition 4]], especially too, as the final event of that impeachment will depend upon a different body, and the members of the house of delegates will be certain, should the decision be ultimately in favor of the president, to become thereby the objects of his displeasure, and to bar to themselves every avenue to the [[☃ definition 5]] of government.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: duty that someone is held accountable for
benefits someone gets from holding office

choices.content: The impeachment process has to get approval from both the judicial and legislative branches, so it will never work.
The impeachment process will most likely not work, as the people responsible for impeachment depend on the president.
The impeachment process is effective because the chambers responsible for carrying it out are elected by the people.
The only way to remove a president is to vote them out of office, which will eventually lead to abuses of powers by sitting presidents.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article by Luther Martin in the Baltimore *Maryland Gazette*. It was published on January 29, 1788.**

It was further observed, that the only appearance of [[☃ definition 1]] in the president, which the system holds up to our view, is the provision for impeachment; but that when we reflect that he cannot be impeached but by the house of delegates, and that the members of this house are [[☃ definition 3]] dependent upon, and unduly under the influence of the president, by being appointable to offices of which he has the sole nomination, so that without his favor and [[☃ definition 2]], they cannot obtain them, there is little reason to believe that a majority will ever [[☃ definition 6]] in impeaching the president, let his conduct be ever so [[☃ definition 4]], especially too, as the final event of that impeachment will depend upon a different body, and the members of the house of delegates will be certain, should the decision be ultimately in favor of the president, to become thereby the objects of his displeasure, and to bar to themselves every avenue to the [[☃ definition 5]] of government.

**Which later development most directly illustrates Luther Martin’s concern that the members of the House of Representatives would be “unduly under the influence of the president”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: duty that someone is held accountable for
benefits someone gets from holding office

choices.content: The creation of the Electoral College
The ratification of the Twenty-Second Amendment
The passage of the War Powers Resolution
The rise of political parties

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1788 article titled “Remarks on the Proposed Plan of a Federal Government” and written under the pseudonym Aristides.**

. . . The power of the president is alarming peculiarly to that class, who cannot bear to view others in possession of that [[☃ definition 1]] blessing, to which, alas! they must themselves aspire in vain. They tell you, this supreme [[☃ definition 2]], although he be called by the modest name of president, and elected for only four years, will, in every essential, be an emperor or king; and that his dignity, in a few years, will become [[☃ definition 3]]. Let us examine the foundation of this alarming prediction.—

Before this appointment can be [[☃ definition 5]], and before even the [[☃ definition 6]] can be enlarged, the constitution must be changed, by consent of the people. By what method, then, shall the president effect this alteration? Every citizen in the union will be a [[☃ definition 4]] on his conduct. Not even his person is particularly protected; and the means of oppression are little in his power. Let the jealousy of the people once take the alarm, and, at the expiration of his term, he is dismissed, as inevitably as light succeeds to darkness.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: passed down from a parent to child automatically
length of time a person can hold office

choices.content: Although the president has significant power, the people will check the power of the presidents and prevent them from serving indefinitely.
The powers given to the president make them too similar to a king, so the government under the Constitution is essentially a monarchy.
Although the president is elected for only four years, the position will be passed down to their family members to keep power concentrated in one family.
The checks on the presidency by the other branches of government make it so the president cannot act without the people’s consent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer*. It was published in 1787 under the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig V.**

In the first place the office of President of the United States appears to me to be clothed with such powers as are dangerous. To be the fountain of all honors in the United States, commander in chief of the army, navy and militia, with the power of making treaties and of granting pardons, and to be [[☃ definition 2]] an authority to put a negative upon all laws, unless two thirds of both houses shall persist in enacting it, and put their names down upon calling the yeas and nays for that purpose, is in reality to be a king as much as *a King as the King of Great Britain,* and a King too of the worst kind;—an elective king. 

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s concerns?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution gives the president too much unchecked power.
There are too many checks on presidential power for the role to be effective.
The election process for the presidency makes the government a monarchy.
The president’s responsibilities are too much for a single person to handle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer*. It was published in 1787 under the [[☃ definition 1]] An American Citizen.**

The judges of the federal courts will owe their appointments to the President and Senate, therefore may not feel so perfectly free from favor, affection and influence as the [[☃ definition 2]], who receive their power from the people, through their state representatives, and are immediately responsible to those assemblies, and finally to the nation at large. Thus we see when a daring or dangerous [[☃ definition 3]] is brought to the [[☃ definition 4]] of public justice, the people who alone can impeach him by their immediate representatives will cause him to be tried, not by the judges appointed in the heat of the occasion, but by two-thirds of a select body, chosen a long time before, for various purposes by the collected wisdom of their state legislatures.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: that is, a president who has committed impeachable offenses

choices.content: Judges of the federal courts should be responsible for conducting impeachment trials, as they are part of the judicial branch.
Senators are bound to follow the wills of their state legislatures and public opinion, which will taint the impeachment process.
The Senate is most likely to hold fair impeachment trials, as they are appointed by the state legislatures and represent the people.
The federal judiciary should offer advice during impeachment trials to ensure the process is legal and follows the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer*. It was published in 1787 under the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig V.**

When I say that our future President will be as much a king as the king of Great-Britain, I only ask of my readers to look into the constitution of that country, and then tell me what [[☃ definition 2]] the King of Great-Britain is entitled to, which does not also belong to the President during his continuance in office. . . . To be tumbled headlong from the [[☃ definition 3]] of greatness and be reduced to a shadow of departed royalty is a shock almost too great for human nature to endure. It will cost a man many struggles to resign such [[☃ definition 4]] powers, and [[☃ definition 6]] long, we shall find, some one who will be very unwilling to part with them. . . . 

So far is it from its being [[☃ definition 5]] that the man who shall hereafter be in a situation to make the attempt to [[☃ definition 7]] his own power, should [[☃ definition 8]] the virtues of General Washington; that it is perhaps a chance of one hundred millions to one that the next age will not furnish an example of so disinterested a use of great power.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s concerns?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The position of president will corrupt even the most virtuous of people, including George Washington.
Any person elected to the presidency will most likely not want to give up the power they have.
The president’s responsibilities are unclear, making the presidency an undesirable position.
Any person to serve as president must act just as George Washington would or lose their position.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer*. It was published in 1787 under the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig V.**

When I say that our future President will be as much a king as the king of Great-Britain, I only ask of my readers to look into the constitution of that country, and then tell me what [[☃ definition 2]] the King of Great-Britain is entitled to, which does not also belong to the President during his continuance in office. . . . To be tumbled headlong from the [[☃ definition 3]] of greatness and be reduced to a shadow of departed royalty is a shock almost too great for human nature to endure. It will cost a man many struggles to resign such [[☃ definition 4]] powers, and [[☃ definition 6]] long, we shall find, some one who will be very unwilling to part with them. . . . 

So far is it from its being [[☃ definition 5]] that the man who shall hereafter be in a situation to make the attempt to [[☃ definition 7]] his own power, should [[☃ definition 8]] the virtues of General Washington; that it is perhaps a chance of one hundred millions to one that the next age will not furnish an example of so disinterested a use of great power.

**Which constitutional amendment would most directly address the concern that the author writes about?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Twelfth Amendment
The Sixteenth Amendment
The Twenty-Second Amendment
The Twenty-Sixth Amendment
